na.summary: Between January and April 2026, the dominant U.S. federal story was the Trump White House's March 20 release of a National Policy Framework for Artificial Intelligence, a sweeping legislative blueprint that calls on Congress to establish a uniform federal standard and preempt conflicting state AI laws. Simultaneously, a bipartisan wave of data-center moratorium bills swept at least 12 state legislatures, driven by ratepayer cost-shifting and grid-reliability concerns, while Sen. Bernie Sanders and Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez introduced a federal companion bill to freeze all AI data-center construction nationally. At the state level, Washington, Utah, and New York enacted new AI laws covering chatbots, content provenance, and frontier-model safety, even as Florida and Virginia ended their sessions without passing any AI legislation. Canada, lacking binding AI law after Bill C-27/AIDA died on the order paper in early 2025, pivoted to infrastructure-first policy, launching a federal call for proposals for sovereign AI data centres exceeding 100 MW and releasing a national 'sprint' report on AI governance priorities.

keyDevelopments.headline: Trump White House Releases National Policy Framework for Artificial Intelligence, Urging Congress to Preempt State AI Laws
Sen. Marsha Blackburn Releases Updated 291-Page Draft of TRUMP AMERICA AI Act, Seeking to Codify Executive Orders and Constrain State AI Regulation
Sanders and Ocasio-Cortez Introduce S.4214, the AI Data Center Moratorium Act, to Halt All New AI Data Center Construction Until Federal Safeguards Are Enacted
At Least 12 States File Data Center Moratorium Bills in 2026 Legislative Sessions, Citing Grid Costs, Water Use, and Ratepayer Protection
Washington Governor Signs HB 2225 (Chatbots) and HB 1170 (AI Provenance Disclosures); New York Governor Hochul Signs RAISE Act Chapter Amendments
Canada Launches Federal Call for Proposals for Sovereign Large-Scale AI Data Centres Exceeding 100 MW, with Submissions Due February 15, 2026
keyDevelopments.source: Innovation, Science and Economic Development Canada (ISED)

eu.summary: Between January and April 2026, the EU's AI regulatory landscape was defined by rapid legislative activity around the Digital Omnibus on AI (COM(2025) 836), which sought to delay and simplify key AI Act deadlines before their August 2026 trigger. The Council and Parliament both adopted negotiating mandates in March 2026 and entered trilogue, targeting a final deal by 28 April. In parallel, the Commission launched a major public consultation on a draft EU-wide Data Centre Rating Scheme and announced a full Data Centre Energy Efficiency Package for Q2 2026. On the international stage, the EU endorsed the New Delhi Declaration at the AI Impact Summit in February 2026, affirming global AI governance cooperation alongside 91 nations.

keyDevelopments.headline: EU Council Adopts Negotiating Mandate on Digital Omnibus on AI (COM/2025/836), Pushing High-Risk AI Act Deadlines to December 2027 and August 2028

asia.summary: From January through April 2026, Asia-Pacific AI and data-center policy was defined by a wave of pro-innovation, sovereignty-focused moves. South Korea became the first country in the region to enforce a comprehensive AI law when its AI Basic Act (Law No. 20676) took effect on January 22, 2026, establishing risk tiers, transparency mandates, and a national AI safety research institute. Japan accelerated its own innovation-first agenda under a newly re-elected Prime Minister Takaichi, completing a public consultation on a Generative AI Code in January and then approving a landmark amendment to its Personal Information Protection Act in April that removes opt-in consent requirements to unlock data for AI training. China extended its vertical control model into 2026, with major CSL amendments bringing AI provisions into national law effective January 1 and the CAC issuing new draft rules on human-like interactive AI services in April. India and Singapore moved more softly: India released principle-based AI Governance Guidelines in February and updated its IT Intermediary Rules to mandate AI content labeling, while Singapore published a first-of-its-kind Model AI Governance Framework for Agentic AI without yet enacting binding legislation.

keyDevelopments.headline: South Korea's AI Basic Act (Law No. 20676) Enters Into Force, Establishing Risk-Tiered Obligations for High-Impact and Generative AI
keyDevelopments.source: Library of Congress / Global Legal Monitor
